query: liver nodules in cirrhotic patients with small enhancing lesions <2cm, diagnostic approach

retrieved_docs.title: Cavernous Hepatic Hemangioma -- Evaluation -- Other Variations Include
retrieved_docs.content: Atypical hemangiomas may appear to enhance in a centrifugal pattern from the inside. In the background of hepatic cirrhosis, the hemangioma may lose the characteristic enhancement pattern, and the flash-filling of small hemangioma can often mimic hepatocellular carcinoma. MRI is 90% sensitive and 91% to 99% specific for diagnosing hepatic hemangiomas. The typical hemangioma appears hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weight images. They are well-circumscribed and homogenous. On postcontrast imaging, the lesions demonstrate the typical peripheral, discontinuous, nodular enhancement with a delayed centripetal filling of the lesion, similar to CT. Smaller hemangiomas demonstrate flash-filling and may mimic hepatocellular carcinoma in the setting of hepatic cirrhosis. Another modality employed is the Technetium-99m pertechnetate-labeled red blood cell scan with single-photon emission CT. There is similar sensitivity to MRI for lesions greater than 1 c,m, but it has not been proven to have the same diagnostic value. Hemangiomas show hypoperfusion or a focal defect during the early dynamic scan, with increased tracer uptake peaking approximately 30 to 50 minutes post-injection. The tracer remains within the lesion on delayed phase images. False negatives may occur secondary to fibrosis or thrombosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Imaging -- Ultrasonography
retrieved_docs.content: A cirrhotic liver can appear hyperechoic on ultrasound. The sensitivity of diagnosing cirrhosis by ultrasound can vary and be as high as 91%. [84] The presence of signs and symptoms of portal hypertension, such as increased spleen or portal vein diameter, can increase the sensitivity of ultrasound in later stages of cirrhosis. [3] A hypervascular nodule in CEUS has a higher specificity for small HCC, less than 2 cm, than CT and MRI studies. Liver metastasis appearance mainly depends on the primary source of the tumor. It can appear as hypoechoic or hyperechoic lesions. The presence of a hyperechoic halo sign is suggestive of a malignant lesion. [85] In CEUS, metastasis has variable echogenicity in the arterial phase, but the washout appearance on the portal phase can help detect malignant lesions. [86]

retrieved_docs.title: Fibrolamellar Hepatocellular Carcinoma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Computed Tomography usually shows a single tumor with well-demarcated margins, with most of these tumors showing decreased attenuation. Greater than 70% of the patients demonstrate a calcified central scar. Liver mass CT protocol commonly involves different intravenously acquisitions at different time points following contrast administration to highlight features in the arterial, portal venous, and delayed phases. Greater than 80% of the patients demonstrate masses that have increased contrast avidity in the arterial phase, reflecting the high blood supply of these tumors. Almost half of these masses enhance similarly to the background liver in the venous phase, and about one-third of the tumors have higher contrast avidity. Almost two-thirds of these masses enhance similar to the background liver on the delayed phase and can be difficult to differentiate. The central fibrous scar in these masses can show contrast avidity in this later phase. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Imaging -- Introduction -- Focal liver lesions generally fall into 3 main clinical categories.
retrieved_docs.content: Benign lesions that do not need treatment if they are asymptomatic, including hepatic hemangiomas, focal nodular hyperplasias, and benign liver cysts Benign lesions that require treatment, including hepatic adenoma, hepatic abscess, and hepatic adenomas Malignant lesions, including hepatocellular carcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma, hepatic angiosarcoma, and liver metastases

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Adenoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of liver lesions suspicious for HCA includes hemangioma, focal nodular hyperplasia (FNH), HCC, and metastatic tumors. Dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI can differentiate between HCA and hemangiomas, which generally exhibit peripheral enhancement and a centripetal fill-in pattern. [21] Gadolinium-based contrast MRI often shows FNH taking up more contrast in the hepatobiliary phase than HCA, along with a central scar and lobulated appearance. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Radiofrequency Ablation of Liver Tumors -- Technique or Treatment -- Contrast-enhanced CT
retrieved_docs.content: A low-density lesion with no contrast enhancement approximately 1 to 3 months following the procedure represents a successful ablation. [27] The shape of the lesion is not considered a significant parameter to differentiate between treatment success or failure. [29] Conversely, thick, nodular, eccentric rim enhancement and/or an increase in the size of the treated lesion are reliable indicators of tumor recurrence and, thus, treatment failure. [30] Four different contrast-enhanced CT recurrence patterns following RFA have been described in the literature: [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Imaging -- Magnetic Resonance
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic hemangioma is the most common benign liver lesion that can be categorized into typical and atypical hemangiomas. A typical hepatic hemangioma can be appreciated on non-contrast CT as a hypo-attenuating lesion most of the time. In arterial phase contrast-enhanced CT, these neoplasms present as a lesion with nodular enhancement in the periphery. Portal venous and delayed phases show continuous enhancement of the periphery with contrast filling the central part. [54] Hemangioma is hypointense on T1 imaging and hyperintense on T2 imaging. Gadolinium-enhanced contrast T1 imaging often shows nodular enhancement of the periphery with a progressive enhancement of the central part and retaining the contrast in delayed imaging. Hepatobiliary contrast T1 imaging is non-specific and may not be helpful. Two main categories for atypical hemangiomas are giant hepatic hemangiomas and flash-filling hepatic hemangiomas. Giant hemangiomas are remarkably large but non-neoplastic vascular lesions with radiologic manifestations similar to hepatic hemangiomas. [55] Flash-filling hepatic hemangiomas/venous malformations are smaller (mostly less than 2 cm) and have a hyperechoic appearance in the ultrasound. [56] They are hypo- or iso-dense in noncontract imaging and demonstrate a fast and homogenous enhancement in the arterial phase while retaining the contrast in the late phase. Flash-filling hemangiomas have similar imaging manifestations in CEUS and gadolinium-enhanced contrast MRI with quick, homogenous enhancement and no washout in the late phase.

retrieved_docs.title: Peliosis Hepatis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographically the lesion can appear similar to many other hepatic lesions. Ranging from 1 to 2 millimeters to centimeters in size and occupying any portion of the liver, it is often mistaken for malignant lesions. To further evaluate, a variety of imaging modalities exist and can be of utmost utility. Angiography often demonstrates contrast accumulating within the parenchyma itself or the sinusoids, depending on when the image is captured. Depending on the degree of involvement and severity of the condition, these findings may or may not be present and often need further advanced imaging with either a triple-phase CT, which can reveal alternating regions of low then high foci, or MRI findings of hypo or hyperintensity based of which weighted image is utilized to observe that there is likely a pooling of blood in these regions. [9] The radiographic differential includes:

retrieved_docs.title: Fibrolamellar Hepatocellular Carcinoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Cavernous hemangiomas follow the enhancement characteristics of blood vessels in all 3 phases. The classic pattern seen is a discontinuous and nodular enhancement pattern in the periphery of these masses in the early phases with subsequent contrast filling of the center. [26] Conventional HCC includes prompt enhancement in the arterial phase with a contrast washout in the delayed phases. These patients often have a cirrhotic liver, which is reflected in the imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Cystic Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Cavernous Hemangioma: are benign tumors of the liver consisting of clusters of blood-filled cavities lined by endothelial cells, supplied by branches of the hepatic artery. On ultrasound, it appears as a hyperechoic homogenous nodule with well-defined margins. it is mostly asymptomatic and requires regular imaging studies. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Focal Nodular Hyperplasia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for focal nodular hyperplasia include the following: Hepatic adenoma Hepatocellular carcinoma Fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma Hemangioma Idiopathic noncirrhotic portal hypertension Regenerative nodules Metastatic disease

retrieved_docs.content: More than 3 tumors (treatment of more lesions may be attempted if successful treatment is felt to be achievable) [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Radiofrequency Ablation of Liver Tumors -- Technique or Treatment -- Contrast-enhanced CT
retrieved_docs.content: Ingrowth: Enhancing tissue inside the border of the treated lesion, denoting inward growth of the tumor. Outgrowth: Enhancing tissue expanding outward from (but contiguous with) the border of the treated lesion, denoting outward growth of the tumor. Spread: New enhancing tissue separate from the treated lesion but contained within the same Couinaud liver segment Progression: New enhancing tissue seen in different Couinaud liver segments from the treated lesion

retrieved_docs.title: Cavernous Hepatic Hemangioma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic hemangiomas can be characterized, and diagnosis can be made by computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), or ultrasound (US). It is important to note that multiple modalities are required for a definitive diagnosis. Percutaneous biopsy is generally not recommended due to the risk of fatal hemorrhage. Diagnosis can be complicated further in the setting of cirrhosis or extra-hepatic malignancy. Thus, additional testing/imaging may be required. [5] [6] [7] Ultrasound demonstrates a homogenous, well-defined hyperechoic mass. If the patient has hepatic steatosis, the hemangioma may appear as hypoechoic. Color Doppler does not add additional diagnostic value. When lesions are larger than 5 cm, some heterogeneity may be demonstrated. Giant hemangiomas are lobulated, heterogeneous masses with a hyperechoic border. The imaging characteristics of hemangiomas on ultrasound are not diagnostic. Therefore, additional imaging is usually required.

retrieved_docs.title: Fibrolamellar Hepatocellular Carcinoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of this disease includes: Focal nodular hyperplasia Giant cavernous hemangioma Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Imaging -- Magnetic Resonance
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is the most common primary liver tumor. Cirrhosis and chronic hepatitis are the major risk factors for the development of HCC. Alpha-fetoprotein becomes elevated in two-thirds of HCC. Elevated alpha-fetoprotein in the presence of cirrhosis is suggestive of HCC and can be seen in 70% of patients with HCC. [65] [66] HCC can appear on ultrasound from focal hypoechoic, hyperechoic, and heterogeneous lesions. Most HCCs are hypervascular on color Doppler. In contrast-enhanced ultrasound, HCC can be appreciated with enhancement in the arterial phase and washout on the portal venous phase. [67] MRI and CT have 82% and 56% sensitivity in detecting HCC, respectively. [68] [69] In CT, the enhancement pattern is similar to contrast-enhanced ultrasound. Because of the possible arterioportal shunt in HCC, focal fatty changes might be seen as focal fat deposition in the normal liver or focal fat sparing around the HCC in diffuse hepatic steatosis. HCC results from a regenerative nodule's progression to dysplastic nodules and high-grade dysplastic nodules to low-grade HCC. The portal vein feeds regenerative and dysplastic nodules, which are indistinguishable from each other on imaging, although dysplastic nodules are premalignant, and regenerative nodules are not. Most regenerative and dysplastic nodules are hypo-intense in T2- T2-weighted images and have variable intensity in T1- T1-weighted images. Regenerative nodules and low-grade dysplastic nodules are isointense in contrast-enhanced MRI. [70] [71]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ). In patients with alcoholic cirrhosis, chest radiographs frequently show atypical patterns that may reflect complications such as pulmonary edema, infections, or pleural effusions, underscoring the importance of evaluating chest imaging when managing this liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: cirrhosis (تشَمُّع كَبِدي) and is associated with esophageal variceal bleeding. The knowledge base therefore integrates congenital correction, diagnostic imaging, endoscopic therapeutic techniques, variceal pathophysiology, and related hepatic risk factors, emphasizing the interconnected role of vascular morphology, procedural categories, and clinical associations in the management of esophageal and related gastrointestinal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: and hepatocellular carcinoma. Hepatocellular carcinoma represents a manifestation of cancer of the liver and intra‑hepatic bile ducts, and it is further related to cholestatic liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ), illustrating a spectrum from malignant to non‑malignant hepatic disorders. Diagnostic and therapeutic procedures targeting the liver include liver biopsy (خزعة الكبد), performed on partially excised hepatic tissue, and ultrasound examination of the liver (فحص الكبد بالموجات فوق الصوتية), which is a child procedure of diagnostic ultrasound of the gastrointestinal tract. The liver also serves as an indirect site for various non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic interventions. Collectively, these interrelated concepts demonstrate the anatomical and functional integration of the gallbladder, liver, and biliary system; the utility of ultrasonographic techniques for biliary and hepatic pathology; the role of substances as causative agents for renal and hepatic toxicities; and the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: non‑operative approach for managing upper GI conditions. The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with chest radiography (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر), emphasizing the role of imaging in the evaluation of these interconnected vascular, hepatic, gastrointestinal and neurological disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal findings on the chest X‑ray (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ). In turn, alcoholic cirrhosis encompasses hepatic steatosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِري) as a related hepatic manifestation. This relationship underscores that radiographic abnormalities of the thorax can reflect underlying alcohol‑related liver disease, and that the progression of alcoholic liver injury often includes fatty infiltration of the liver (hepatic steatosis) before or alongside the development of cirrhosis. Recognizing these connections aids clinicians in linking chest imaging findings to potential liver pathology and in monitoring the spectrum of alcohol‑induced hepatic damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 195
retrieved_docs.content: abdominal hernia (فتق البطن), and biliary disorders (اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ). Clinical findings such as an abdominal mass (كتلة في البَطْن) or acute abdomen (بَطْنٌ حادّ) are evaluated with the same imaging and procedural approaches. Understanding the interconnected anatomy of the abdomen and liver, the spectrum of hepatic diseases, their risk factors, and the potential toxicities of substances used in abdominal interventions is essential for safe procedural planning, accurate diagnosis, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 72

Overall, the knowledge base integrates congenital esophageal repair, endoscopic dilatation, variceal management, lower‑extremity venous disease, and their common etiologic factors (alcohol, liver disease) to guide comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: disease include liver biopsy (خزعة الكبد), partially excised liver anatomy procedures, and ultrasound‑guided examinations, all classified under diagnostic ultrasound of the gastrointestinal tract or of the abdomen/retroperitoneum. The liver also functions as an indirect site for non‑gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures. Overall, the network highlights the interrelationships among abdominal procedures, biliary imaging, liver pathology, detoxification therapies, and their diagnostic modalities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell carcinoma), which is broadly related to مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ (jaundiced liver disease). This connection indicates that hepatocellular carcinoma often presents within the spectrum of liver diseases that cause jaundice, highlighting the importance of recognizing jaundice as a potential clinical clue to underlying malignant liver pathology. Understanding this relationship aids clinicians in early detection, accurate diagnosis, and appropriate management of patients with liver‑related malignancies and associated jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 684
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي, ضغط الدم) is a primary form of high blood pressure characterized by elevated systolic, diastolic, and mean arterial pressures. It is clinically similar to benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis and shares renal vascular changes, while the more severe malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is linked to the broader category “hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension.” Essential hypertension includes the subtypes diastolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) and systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي), the latter being a vascular disorder within the vascular domain. The condition may progress to hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم) and is associated with primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ). Abnormal chest radiographs (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) can be observed in patients with essential hypertension and are also associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ), indicating that hepatic disease may produce atypical pulmonary imaging findings. The vascular system therefore encompasses elevated pressure measurements, hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, diastolic and systolic hypertension, and their renal and hepatic interrelationships, informing comprehensive assessment and management of patients with hypertension and related vascular, renal, and hepatic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2251
retrieved_docs.content: The liver, referred to as játra, can function as an indirect site for a range of other non‑or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures, indicating that it may be affected or involved in treatments that are not primarily directed at the gastrointestinal system. In contrast, the liver is also a direct site for the diagnostic procedure فحص الكبد بالموجات فوق الصوتية (ultrasound examination of the liver), which specifically targets hepatic tissue for imaging and assessment. This dual role underscores the liver’s relevance both as a secondary participant in broader therapeutic interventions and as a primary focus of dedicated hepatic imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: SMQ. Hepatic cirrhosis and portal hypertension predispose patients to دوالي المَريء, which can progress to نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding). This bleeding is a major cause of نَزِف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي (upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage) and is linked to نزيف الدوالي الوريدية (variceal bleeding). Overall, the framework links esophageal variceal pathology, its underlying vascular morphology, associated bleeding manifestations, alcohol‑related liver disease, portal hypertension, and the endoscopic therapeutic procedures used for diagnosis, classification, and patient‑centered management.
